content: This protocol draft outlines a neurostimulation treatment plan for attention-related conditions using transcranial direct current stimulation. The clinical rationale is based on evidence of frontal hypoactivation patterns. The protocol targets the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex at 2 mA for 20 min sessions. Safety considerations include seizure history screening and current medication review. All session parameters include appropriate unit annotations. The evidence base supports this approach with indicative confidence level.

sections.body: The clinical rationale for this neurostimulation protocol is based on convergent evidence from quantitative EEG and neuroimaging studies demonstrating frontal hypoactivation patterns in attention-deficit presentations. Specifically, elevated theta/beta ratio at frontal electrode sites (Fz, F3, F4) and reduced prefrontal cortical activation during sustained attention tasks support the targeting of left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex with anodal transcranial direct current stimulation. Multiple randomized controlled trials have demonstrated improvements in attention metrics following frontal tDCS protocols in this population, providing an evidence base rated as moderate by systematic review criteria.

This protocol is indicated for adults aged 18-65 years with diagnosed attention deficit conditions confirmed by comprehensive neuropsychological assessment. Eligible participants must demonstrate qEEG patterns consistent with frontal hypoactivation including elevated theta/beta ratio above 2.5 at Fz. Participants must have completed baseline cognitive testing including continuous performance tasks and working memory assessments. Concurrent psychotropic medication is permitted if stable for at least 4 weeks prior to enrollment. Participants must provide written informed consent and be able to attend scheduled sessions.

The following exclusion criteria apply: (1) History of seizure disorder or epilepsy. (2) Presence of intracranial metallic implants, cochlear implants, or cardiac pacemakers. (3) Active substance use disorder within the past 6 months. (4) Pregnancy or planned pregnancy during the treatment period. (5) Severe traumatic brain injury with loss of consciousness exceeding 30 minutes. (6) Active psychotic symptoms or unstable psychiatric condition. (7) Skin conditions at electrode placement sites that would compromise contact quality.

Absolute contraindications include: active seizure disorder, intracranial metallic hardware, and implanted electrical devices (pacemakers, deep brain stimulators). Relative contraindications requiring clinician judgment include: family history of epilepsy, concurrent use of medications that lower seizure threshold (e.g., bupropion, clozapine), recent changes in psychotropic medication within 2 weeks, significant skin lesions at electrode sites, and history of adverse reactions to transcranial electrical stimulation. All contraindications must be screened at each session prior to stimulation delivery.

Safety monitoring includes pre-session screening questionnaire, continuous observation during stimulation, and post-session adverse event assessment. Common side effects include mild tingling or itching at electrode sites (reported in 40-60% of sessions), mild headache (10-15%), and transient skin redness at electrode sites. Serious adverse events are rare but include new-onset seizure (estimated risk less than 1 in 10000 sessions based on published safety data). Emergency stop procedures must be immediately available. Stimulation must be discontinued immediately if the participant reports severe discomfort, visual disturbances, or any neurological symptoms. A qualified clinician must be present for all sessions.

Stimulation parameters: modality is transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS). Anode placement at F3 (left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex) using 10-20 system coordinates. Cathode placement at right supraorbital region (Fp2). Electrode size: 25 cm2 saline-soaked sponge electrodes. Current intensity: 2 mA. Current density: 0.08 mA/cm2. Ramp up: 30 seconds. Stimulation duration: 20 min per session. Ramp down: 30 seconds. Session frequency: 3 sessions per week. Total planned sessions: 15 sessions over 5 weeks. Inter-session interval: minimum 24 hours between sessions.

Primary outcome measures include: (1) Change in theta/beta ratio at Fz measured by qEEG at baseline, mid-treatment (session 8), and post-treatment (session 15). (2) Performance change on the Continuous Performance Test-3 (CPT-3) measuring sustained attention, omission errors, and reaction time variability. Secondary outcome measures include: (3) Self-reported attention symptoms using the Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS-v1.1). (4) Clinician-rated symptom improvement using the Clinical Global Impression-Improvement scale (CGI-I). All assessments are conducted at baseline, mid-treatment, and 4-week follow-up.

Ongoing monitoring includes: pre-session vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate), pre-session mood and adverse event screening using standardized questionnaire, real-time impedance monitoring during stimulation to ensure electrode contact quality, post-session adverse event assessment within 15 minutes of session completion, and weekly safety review by the supervising clinician. A formal mid-treatment review at session 8 will evaluate preliminary response and determine continuation versus protocol modification. Data monitoring committee review is not required for this clinical protocol given the established safety profile of tDCS at the specified parameters.

Participants must provide written informed consent using the approved consent document (Form IC-tDCS-ATT-v2.1) prior to enrollment. The consent process includes detailed explanation of the procedure, expected benefits and risks, alternative treatments, right to withdraw at any time without consequence, and data handling procedures. A comprehension check questionnaire must be completed satisfactorily before consent is finalized. Consent is obtained by a trained study coordinator and verified by the supervising clinician. Re-consent is required if there are any protocol amendments affecting participant risk.

The evidence supporting frontal tDCS for attention-related conditions includes five randomized controlled trials with sample sizes ranging from 20 to 64 participants. Meta-analytic findings suggest moderate effect sizes for attention improvement (Hedges g = 0.42, 95% CI 0.18-0.66). The evidence grade is classified as moderate based on the GRADE framework, with points deducted for inconsistency across studies and imprecision due to small sample sizes. Neurophysiological evidence from qEEG studies demonstrates that tDCS can modulate frontal theta activity, supporting the mechanistic rationale. The overall confidence level for clinical benefit is indicative.

Limitations of this protocol include: (1) The evidence base, while growing, is primarily derived from small-sample studies with limited long-term follow-up data. (2) Individual response variability to tDCS is well-documented and predicted response rates based on qEEG profiles remain under investigation. (3) Blinding integrity in tDCS trials is challenged by the sensory experience of active stimulation. (4) The optimal stimulation parameters (intensity, duration, electrode montage) for this population are not yet definitively established. (5) This protocol does not include a sham control condition and therefore cannot isolate placebo effects from active treatment effects.

citations.title: Transcranial direct current stimulation for ADHD: A systematic review and meta-analysis
